topic: Southern Distance Quadratic Latitude Law — post-c2 rewrite integration
change_summary: detail_evidence 621→5142 chars (728%), detail_verdict_text 116→952 chars (821%), finding/claim fields populated. Decider integrated most c2 recommendations (Sydney–Santiago kill-shot, Vincenty/Karney citations, V50.6 supersession acknowledged, adjusted R² computed, code_analysis tags corrected). Verdict unchanged (Misleading).
our_argument_summary: The rewritten WIN-027 acknowledges the kernel of truth (AE projection distortion), admits the dome itself superseded the quadratic via V50.6 H(r), shows that H(r) is equally post-hoc (4 fitted parameters against 19 pairs), computes adjusted R² ≈ 0.75, and adds the Sydney–Santiago kill-shot (~30,000 km disc chord vs 11,338 km globe). The structure is much stronger than c2. A standardized post-hoc boilerplate paragraph was also appended to detail_verdict_text.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: c2's findings still hold: find_curve.py skeleton with 6 hardcoded cities and empty body; no repository script performs the claimed 20-route regression. Values appear only in raw-text notes and wins.html display strings. The new text makes these structural gaps explicit.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded throughout. The quadratic coefficients, the R²=0.7874, the V50.6 H₀=8537/λ=8619 parameters, and the 5.2% / 0.0% headline numbers are all static strings. No script computes them from route data.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome can retreat to the V50.6 H(r) mechanism and present 5.2% / 0.0% numbers as the real prediction, treating WIN-027's quadratic as historical. The new text preempts this retreat explicitly by evaluating both stages and identifying H(r) as equally post-hoc.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None detected in the new text; the find_curve.py / wins.html claims are preserved from c2 and remain accurate against the raw-text dump.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py domain for WIN-027. Quadratic coefficients and R² appear only as static assertions in raw-text/02-wins.txt and the rendered wins.html — no fetch logic, no regression routine in find_curve.py.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The quadratic distortion the dome fits is exactly the distortion signature of the azimuthal-equidistant projection from a sphere onto a plane. Standard cartography predicts this pattern; the dome renames it a 'law'.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Raw-text evolution note (02-wins.txt): 'v22 found circular disc fails on southern/cross-equator distances. That was correct. WIN-027 (quadratic law) and WIN-030 (ellipse) directly address this.' The quadratic was invented to patch an admitted failure of the base geometry.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation path from disc_radius or firmament_height to coefficients 0.00131, 0.06828, 1.06719. The values are stated as a regression output without the regression, on a route set that is nowhere enumerated.

holes_found.description: Arithmetic error in the kill-shot paragraph: '5.2% mean error (≈520 m per km)'. 5.2% of 1 km is 52 m, not 520 m. The parenthetical is off by a factor of 10. A dome defender who spots this (easy — it's a direct arithmetic check) can use it to discredit the whole section: 'The critics cannot do basic percent-to-metres conversion.' The adjacent claim '5.2% becomes hundreds of kilometres' at trans-hemispheric range is correct (5.2% × 10,000 km = 520 km), which makes the '520 m per km' error especially glaring — the author likely intended '520 m per 10 km' or simply wrote the trans-hemispheric number in the wrong row.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace '(≈520 m per km)' with '(≈52 m per km, or 520 m over a 10 km leg, 520 km over a 10,000 km trans-hemispheric route)' — OR drop the parenthetical entirely and rely on 'At trans-hemispheric range that 5.2% becomes hundreds of kilometres' which is correct.

holes_found.description: tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict both claim 'the dome's best formula still has 21% unexplained error' / '21% unexplained error is enormous'. But the expanded detail_evidence explicitly states the dome has SUPERSEDED the quadratic with V50.6 H(r), which the dome reports as 5.2% mean error across 19 pairs — a different metric and a much smaller number. The TLDR leads a casual reader to believe the dome's current best model has 21% unexplained variance, which contradicts our own detail text. Even though our point is that the quadratic IS the item under review (and V50.6 is equally post-hoc), the TLDR mispresents where the dome currently stands. A dome defender reading only the TLDR will respond: 'You are attacking an abandoned V50.4 formula — our current model claims 5.2% error, not 21%.'
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite tldr_evidence: 'The dome found something real: projecting a sphere onto a flat disc creates systematic distance distortions. The V50.4 quadratic the dome presents here was superseded by V50.6 H(r), which still needs four fitted parameters against 19 cherry-picked pairs — while standard spherical geodesy needs two parameters and achieves sub-meter precision globally. The distortion pattern matches the azimuthal equidistant projection, a known map projection, not new physics.' Mirror the same framing in tldr_verdict.

holes_found.description: Cross-WIN parameter-count inconsistency. WIN-027 enumerates the V50.6 free parameters as '(amplitude 8,537 and scale length 8,619) plus an east–west angular scale factor of 0.9941, plus an ellipse axis ratio (WIN-030) — four free parameters.' WIN-057 enumerates them as '(H₀, λ in H(r) = H₀·exp(−r/λ), EW angular scale 0.9941, and equatorial ring radius 14,105 km) — four fitted parameters.' Both say four, but the sets differ (WIN-027 includes the ellipse ratio, omits 14,105; WIN-057 includes 14,105, omits the ellipse ratio). The true union is at least five (H₀, λ, 0.9941, ellipse ratio, 14,105) — possibly six if you count disc_radius = 20,015. A defender collating both WINs can claim we double-count or miscount, and weaken our parsimony argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Pick one canonical parameter list and use it in both WINs, or restate as 'at least five fitted parameters (H₀, λ, EW scale 0.9941, ellipse axis ratio, equatorial ring radius 14,105 km; possibly six if the disc radius 20,015 km is also treated as fitted)'. Consistency matters more than the exact count.

holes_found.description: The Vincenty accuracy description '(≈0.5 mm per km, <0.001% relative)' pairs a per-km rate with a relative-error bound. Vincenty/Karney error is bounded in ABSOLUTE terms (sub-nanometre per Karney 2013 for distances up to ~10^9 km), not as a per-km rate — the error doesn't accumulate linearly with distance. Stating accuracy as '0.5 mm per km' invites a defender to ask 'so at 10,000 km you have 5 m of error?' when in fact the absolute error remains sub-millimetre regardless of distance. The two numbers happen to be mutually consistent arithmetically (5×10⁻⁷ fractional << 10⁻⁵ fractional = 0.001%) but the framing misrepresents how geodesic algorithm error behaves.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace '(≈0.5 mm per km, <0.001% relative)' with '(sub-millimetre absolute error on the full WGS84 ellipsoid, per Karney 2013 — accuracy does not degrade with distance)'. This also pre-empts any 'your geodesy benchmark is unfair to the dome' line of attack.

holes_found.description: The QF27/QF28 'nonstop' framing is fragile. Qantas has operated SYD–SCL in both nonstop and via-Auckland configurations across different schedules; a defender who knows the current operating pattern can say 'QF27 stops in Auckland' and weaken the rhetorical punch. The underlying kill-shot (commercial subsonic aircraft covering ~30,000 km in ~13 hours is impossible) stands regardless of stop structure, because even with a refuelling stop the total in-air time is inconsistent with a 30,000 km route.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften the specificity: replace 'Qantas QF27/QF28 ... nonstop at subsonic cruise speed' with 'Direct Sydney–Santiago services (Qantas and LATAM have each operated the corridor) complete the 11,338 km great-circle in ~13 hours of in-air time. A 30,000 km disc route would need ~36 hours at 800 km/h — longer than any southern-hemisphere flight segment ever scheduled.'

holes_found.description: Detail_evidence opens with 'The kernel of truth' immediately after the HTML <strong> tag, then uses 'The irony is that this signature is the opposite of what the dome claims: the correction exists because the Earth is curved, not because it is flat.' This wording subtly concedes that the surface curves while asserting the dome is wrong. A pedantic defender could object: 'You say the Earth is curved as a premise, then use that to refute our model — circular.' The argument is actually valid (the quadratic distortion signature is intrinsic to sphere-to-plane projection regardless of the ontological question), but the phrasing invites the charge.
holes_found.recommendation: Rephrase as 'the correction exists because any sphere-to-plane projection of a round surface produces exactly this distortion pattern — the dome has rediscovered the mathematical fingerprint of the geometry it is trying to replace.'

holes_found.description: detail_verdict_text appends the standardized post-hoc boilerplate paragraph ('Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test...'). For WIN-027 specifically the boilerplate is redundant with the preceding argument — the detail_evidence already makes the post-hoc case with more specificity (evolution note, v22 failure, V50.4→V50.6 progression). Two paragraphs saying the same thing in different voices reads as boilerplate contamination.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) drop the boilerplate when the detail already covers post-hoc extensively, or (b) trim the boilerplate to one sentence and anchor-link to Part 3.1b for the full argument.

stronger_arguments.description: The V50.6 H(r) coordinate conversion r(city) = solve(r·tan(polaris_elevation) = H(r)) explicitly takes Polaris elevation as input. Polaris elevation ≈ latitude on a sphere — it is a globe observable the dome cannot derive without the round Earth. WIN-027's text mentions this but does not spell it out. The strongest form of the circularity argument is: 'The dome's distance mechanism requires latitude as input; latitude is a globe coordinate; therefore the dome's distance mechanism is parasitic on globe coordinates.' This mirrors the WGS-84 dependency argument in WIN-058.

The dome's 0.0% Oslo–Stockholm result is not just rhetorically cherry-picked — it is a mathematical identity at near-equal latitudes. When lat1 ≈ lat2, the disc chord formula d = √(r₁² + r₂² − 2r₁r₂·cos(Δθ)) simplifies to ~2r·sin(Δθ/2), which is the same shape as the spherical chord formula 2R·sin(Δθ/2) scaled by r/R. The EW scale factor 0.9941 is chosen so that these two expressions coincide at the latitude of the best-fit pair. 0.0% error at Oslo–Stockholm is therefore trivially guaranteed once the scale factor is fit — it is not evidence of anything. This is a sharper kill than 'cherry-picked': the success is algebraically forced.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome correctly identifies that mapping a sphere onto a disc produces systematic, latitude-dependent distance distortion, and the approximate quadratic shape of this distortion at mid-to-southern latitudes is a real mathematical pattern (the AE projection signature). Recognizing the pattern is a legitimate observation — fitting a correction factor to it is legitimate curve-fitting.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The distortion the dome fits is the signature of the round-Earth geometry being forced onto a plane. A quadratic correction is the fingerprint of spherical curvature. Claiming this signature as a 'dome prediction' is the dome rediscovering the curvature it denies, then labelling the rediscovery a win. The kernel proves the opposite of what the dome claims.

deeper_analysis:
The post-c2 rewrite is a genuine improvement. The three critical/major c2 holes (supersession acknowledgement, Sydney–Santiago kill-shot, Vincenty comparison with concrete numbers) are all addressed. Adjusted R² ≈ 0.75 (independently verified: (1-0.7874)·19/16 = 0.2526 → 0.7474) is now in the text. The boilerplate post-hoc paragraph was appended, which is consistent with a site-wide standardization pass rather than a WIN-027-specific edit.

The remaining holes are the '520 m per km' arithmetic error (a clear factor-of-10 mistake that will jump out at any numerate reader) and the TLDR/detail impedance mismatch (TLDR attacks the quadratic, detail acknowledges V50.6 as the dome's current best). Neither undermines the verdict, but both create dome-defender ammunition. The cross-WIN parameter-count discrepancy with WIN-057 (different 'four parameters' on each page) is a consistency defect that should be fixed in both WINs at once.

Verdict 'Misleading' remains appropriate. Confidence 0.88 (down from c2's 0.91) because the new text introduces new potential defects (arithmetic error, TLDR/detail mismatch, cross-WIN parameter inconsistency) while fixing older ones — net improvement but with new surface area.

citations_verified: Vincenty 1975 DOI 10.1179/sre.1975.23.176.88 — correct reference, Survey Review 22(176)
Karney 2013 arXiv:1109.4448 — correct preprint ID for 'Algorithms for geodesics'
Sydney–Santiago great-circle 11,338 km — verified independently: haversine gives 11,346 km (within rounding)
Adjusted R² ≈ 0.75 — verified: (1-0.7874)(20-1)/(20-3-1)?; using n=20, p=3 formula = 0.747
Disc chord Sydney–Santiago ~30,000 km — verified: linear colat mapping r(lat) with r_eq=14,105, disc_radius=20,015 gives d ≈ 30,476 km
citations_failed: '5.2% mean error (≈520 m per km)' — arithmetic is wrong by factor 10; should be ~52 m per km
citations_unchecked: Qantas QF27/QF28 current operating status (nonstop vs via AKL) — depends on current schedule; argument holds either way

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender who has absorbed the six methodological principles could mount this defense: (1) The critics' own detail admits V50.6 H(r) achieves 5.2% mean error, yet their TLDR still waves '21% unexplained' as if the quadratic is our current mechanism — they are attacking a V50.4 formula we ourselves superseded, which undermines the 'no default favoritism' principle. (2) The critics say our parameter count makes H(r) less parsimonious than Vincenty, but in WIN-027 they count four parameters one way and in WIN-057 they count four parameters a different way — which is it? The count is a rhetorical device, not a measurement. (3) The reviewers' kill-shot paragraph contains '520 m per km' when 5.2% of 1 km is 52 m — a tenfold arithmetic error in a paragraph whose whole point is precision. If the critics cannot do the arithmetic in the paragraph they themselves write, why should anyone trust their numerical critique of our model?
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: All three prongs are fixable by the edits already listed in holes_found: correct the '520 m per km' arithmetic (fixes prong 3, the most damaging), align the TLDR with the detail (fixes prong 1), and make the parameter counts consistent across WIN-027 and WIN-057 (fixes prong 2). Until those three edits land, defense_survives stays high because the defender doesn't need to argue physics — they can discredit us on basic quality control. This is precisely the 'bulletproof every claim' mandate of the curmudgeon role.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-027 and WIN-057 give DIFFERENT lists of 'four fitted parameters' for the same V50.6 H(r) distance mechanism. WIN-027: (H₀, λ, EW scale 0.9941, ellipse axis ratio). WIN-057: (H₀, λ, EW scale 0.9941, equatorial ring radius 14,105). The true union is at least five parameters. A dome defender can compile the two pages and claim we either double-count or miscount. Fix both pages together — pick one canonical enumeration. Separately, WIN-027 and WIN-058 make mutually-reinforcing arguments about globe-coordinate parasitism (longitude for WIN-058, latitude via Polaris elevation for WIN-027), but WIN-027 does not cross-link to WIN-058 and vice versa — a missed opportunity to build a cumulative case.

claims_checked: 5.2% of 1 km = 52 m (text says 520 m → factor-of-10 error)
Sydney–Santiago great-circle = 11,346 km (text 11,338 km, within rounding)
Disc chord Sydney–Santiago with r_eq=14,105, disc_radius=20,015, linear colat mapping, Δθ=138.12° = 30,476 km (text ~30,000)
Adjusted R² with n=20, p=3, R²=0.7874 = 0.7475 (text ~0.75)
Vincenty 0.5 mm per km relative = 5×10⁻⁷ < 10⁻⁵ = 0.001% (two framings arithmetically consistent but per-km framing is misleading for geodesic algorithms)
quantitative_verification.computations: Python haversine for great-circle, planar law of cosines for disc chord using linear colat→r mapping, standard adj-R² formula. All numerical claims in the detail_evidence verify within rounding EXCEPT the '520 m per km' parenthetical, which is factor-of-10 too large.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: '5.2% mean error (≈520 m per km)' is arithmetically wrong; correct value is ≈52 m per km. The adjacent 'at trans-hemispheric range that 5.2% becomes hundreds of kilometres' IS correct (5.2% × 10,000 km = 520 km), which suggests the author mis-scaled when converting the trans-hemispheric figure into a per-km rate.
